steps.title: Compare strangers and ties
steps.content: Text 1 says Kaya and colleagues demonstrated a benefit from making connections with strangers: participants who warmly thanked a shuttle driver reported greater positive affect than participants who did not speak to the driver. Text 2 describes a social-network model made of strong ties, close but less important ties, and weak ties.

steps.title: Point out the missing category
steps.content: Because the model in Text 2 still centers relationships counted as part of a person's social network, Kaya and colleagues would likely note that it leaves out stranger interactions like the shuttle-driver exchange. Choice B focuses on relationship longevity, which is not the main issue. Choice C claims familiar interactions are expected to be more positive. Choice D concerns distinctions among close connections, not the missing category of strangers. The answer is A.
